g.data-narration: Egrep stands for Extended Grep. It searches text using extended regular expressions with powerful metacharacters.
g.text: Extended grep supports metacharacters: + ? | () without escaping

g.data-narration: Essential metacharacters form the foundation of regex patterns. Each symbol has specific meaning in pattern matching.
Character classes define sets of characters to match. Use brackets with characters inside for custom matching sets.
Quantifiers specify how many times a pattern should match. Use curly braces for exact or range-based repetition.
Example: Find lines starting with Error or WARNING using caret and alternation. This is useful for log analysis.
g.text: ^ anchors to start | (Error|WARNING) matches either word

g.data-narration: Example: Match phone numbers in XXX-XXX-XXXX format using digit quantifiers. Each group matches exactly 3 digits.
g.text: [0-9]{3} matches exactly 3 digits | hyphens match literally

g.data-narration: Example: Extract email addresses using character classes for username, domain, and top-level domain matching.
Example: Find words with exactly two consecutive vowels using quantifier {2}. Great for linguistic analysis.
g.text: [aeiou]{2} finds any two vowels in a row like 'ea', 'io', 'ou'

g.data-narration: Example: Extract IPv4 addresses using grouped quantifiers. Each octet matches 1-3 digits followed by a period.
g.text: Three octets with dots, plus final octet | {1,3} matches 1 to 3 digits

g.data-narration: Example: Find repeated consecutive words using backreferences. \1 refers back to the captured word group.
g.text: \b = word boundary | (\w+) captures word | \1 backreference to first group

g.data-narration: Example: Match dates in DD-MM-YYYY or YYYY-MM-DD format with proper validation for day and month ranges.
Key exam tips: Remember the difference between basic and extended grep, practice common patterns, and use proper anchoring.
Summary: Egrep uses extended regex with metacharacters. Master character classes, quantifiers, and backreferences for effective pattern matching.
Practice these patterns to master egrep. Regular expressions are essential for text processing in Unix systems and programming.
Thank you for learning egrep and regular expressions. Practice these examples to become proficient in pattern matching.
g.text: Master regular expressions for text processing, log analysis, and data extraction
